events.text: [She's surrounded by books. All of them open. None of them to the same page. She looks like someone who has read everything and is still not done.]
And you've decided that love isn't evidence of anything.

choices.text: Love is chemistry. It's not evidence of God.
I don't see how that's relevant.
What do you think it's evidence of?

events.label: choice_chemistry
events.text: I'm not arguing for God. I'm arguing for love as a phenomenon that resists reduction.
You can explain attraction with oxytocin. You cannot explain why this specific person makes you feel like you've finally been understood.

choices.text: I know it might not be real.

events.label: chem_projection
events.text: Maybe. But you've built an entire cosmology to justify what the projection means to you.
That's not chemistry. That's faith. Just pointed in a different direction.
That gap — between knowing and still feeling — that's the thing I call sacred. It doesn't need a name beyond that.

events.label: choice_deflect
events.text: Everything is relevant when you're deciding whether the world should exist.
You want to end it, and the only thing giving you pause is a person. I find that interesting.

events.label: choice_open
events.text: Something you can't control. Something that arrived without being invited and refuses to be reasoned away.
I call that transcendence. You might call it a flaw in your argument. Either way, it's there.

choices.text: I don't know what to do with that.
That still doesn't mean anything matters.

events.label: open_uncertain
events.text: That's honest. Most people try to resolve it too quickly.
Maybe you don't have to do anything with it. Maybe you just have to let it be true.

events.label: open_push
events.text: You're right. It doesn't. Not in the cosmic sense.
But it matters to you. And you matter to her. That's a small chain of meaning. Tiny, fragile, real.
